stem: A large data set is approximately normally distributed and contains \( [[N]] \) values. By the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule), about how many of the values lie within \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) of the mean?
solution: Within \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) the empirical rule keeps about \( [[p]]/1000 \) of the data, so the count is \( \frac{[[p]]}{1000}\cdot[[N]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts the values OUTSIDE \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) instead of inside.
This uses the 1-standard-deviation proportion (about 68%) regardless of the stated number of standard deviations.
This uses the 2-standard-deviation proportion (about 95%) regardless of the stated number of standard deviations.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: A large data set is approximately normally distributed and contains \( [[N]] \) values. By the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule), about how many of the values lie MORE than \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) away from the mean (i.e., outside that range on either side)?
solution: The empirical rule keeps about \( [[p]]/1000 \) of the data within \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s), so the remaining fraction outside that range is \( \frac{1000-[[p]]}{1000}\cdot[[N]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count of values INSIDE \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s), not outside.
This always uses the 1-standard-deviation tail fraction, regardless of the stated number of standard deviations.
This always uses the 2-standard-deviation tail fraction, regardless of the stated number of standard deviations.
This claims every value in the data set lies outside the range.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: A large data set is approximately normally distributed and contains \( [[N]] \) values. By the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule), about how many of the values lie MORE than \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) ABOVE the mean (the upper tail alone)?
solution: The two tails together hold \( \frac{1000-[[p]]}{1000} \) of the data; by symmetry the upper tail alone holds half of that: \( \frac{1000-[[p]]}{2000}\cdot[[N]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is BOTH tails combined; it forgets to split the outside proportion in half by symmetry.
This mistakenly halves the proportion INSIDE the range instead of the proportion outside it.
This uses the proportion inside the range instead of the outer-tail proportion.
This counts everything except the upper tail, rather than the upper tail itself.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: A large data set is approximately normally distributed and contains \( [[N]] \) values. By the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule), about how many of the values lie between \( [[k1]] \) and \( [[k2]] \) standard deviations ABOVE the mean?
solution: The proportion within \( [[k2]] \) SDs minus the proportion within \( [[k1]] \) SDs gives both bands beyond \( [[k1]] \); by symmetry the upper band alone is half of that: \( \frac{[[p2]]-[[p1]]}{2000}\cdot[[N]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to split the band into an upper and lower half by symmetry.
This uses only the outer boundary's proportion, ignoring the inner cutoff entirely.
This uses only the inner boundary's proportion, ignoring the outer cutoff entirely.
This adds the two boundary proportions instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: A large data set is approximately normally distributed and contains \( [[N]] \) values. By the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule), about how many of the values lie BELOW the point that is \( [[k]] \) standard deviation(s) ABOVE the mean?
solution: Half the data lies below the mean, plus half of the \( [[p]]/1000 \) that lies within \( [[k]] \) SDs (the half above the mean): \( \frac{1000+[[p]]}{2000}\cdot[[N]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes the proportion below a point \( [[k]] \) SDs BELOW the mean instead of above it.
This uses only the 'within \( [[k]] \) SDs' proportion and forgets the entire lower half of the data.
This makes an arithmetic slip combining the 50% lower half with the tail proportion instead of the within-range proportion.
This computes the proportion ABOVE the cutoff instead of below it.
This is off by one from the correct count.
